data.title: Kimberly-Clark to Pay Up to $40 Million to Resolve Criminal Charge Tied to Sale of Surgical Gowns
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Kimberly-Clark has agreed to pay up to $40.4 million to resolve a criminal charge relating to its sale of adulterated surgical gowns.

The Department of Justice said Thursday that a Kimberly-Clark employee conducted fraudulent testing on the company's MicroCool gowns to avoid submitting a premarket notification to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration after the company made a change to the gowns.

Kimberly-Clark continued to sell the gowns after the change without a new premarket notification, marketing the gowns as providing the highest level of protection against fluid and viruses, the DOJ said.

In total, Kimberly-Clark sold about $49 million worth of adulterated MicroCool gowns to customers in the U.S. and abroad, the DOJ said.

A U.S. District Court charged the Dallas-based company with one count of introducing adulterated surgical gowns into interstate commerce with an intent to defraud and mislead.

Premarket notifications aim to show the FDA that a medical device is as safe and effective as an already legally marketed device, the DOJ said. For U.S.-sold surgical gowns intended for surgeries and other high-risk medical procedures, the FDA requires that fluids be prevented from penetrating the gown.

The payment from Kimberly-Clark is to comprise a monetary penalty of $24.5 million, a forfeiture of $3.9 million in profit and up to $12 million in victim compensation.

As part of the resolution, the company has agreed to strengthen its compliance program and abide by reporting requirements.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Cooper Cos. Solid Margins Should Boost Stock Despite Earnings Disappointment — Market Talk
data.description: Cooper Cos.'s solid margins and improving free cash flow should help the company's stock despite the company's poor third-quarter earnings, say Stifel analysts, who label the stock as a "buy." The company's adjusted operating margin of 26.1% beat Wall Street estimates and Cooper said operating efficiencies are expected to continue through a restructuring in 2026. Free cash flow also beat expectations by a wide margin with working-capital efficiencies expected to continue bolstering the company's cash in coming years. The stock is down 11%, to $66.27, Thursday. That decline gives investors an opportunity to buy, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Cathie Wood’s Ark Invest Buys $15.6M BMNR – Bet on Tom Lee’s ETH Treasury?
data.description:
Cathie Wood’s ARK Invest expanded its crypto exposure this week with a fresh $15.6 million purchase of BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), despite the stock closing down 7.85% on the day.

According to ARK’s daily trading update on August 27, 339,113 shares of BitMine across three of its exchange-traded funds.

Cathie Wood and Ark Invest bought 339,113 shares of Tom Lee's today — Tom Lee Tracker (@TomLeeTracker)

The ARK Innovation ETF added 227,569 shares worth $10.48 million; the Next Generation Internet ETF picked up 70,991 shares valued at $3.27 million; and the Fintech Innovation ETF bought 40,553 shares for $1.87 million.

BMNR closed at $46.03 before slipping another 7.85% in after-hours trading, according to Google Finance.Cathie Wood Shows Confidence in Ethereum With Fresh BitMine Buys

The investment builds on ARK’s earlier accumulation of BitMine stock, which has surpassed $200 million in purchases this summer.

The decision shows Wood’s growing confidence in BitMine’s Ethereum-focused strategy, even as the firm continues to adjust its portfolios by trimming holdings in Coinbase, Roblox, and other fintech names.

Late last month, ARK offloaded $90.5 million worth of Coinbase shares and $57.7 million in Roblox stock, while adding exposure to BitMine as well as companies like AMD, DoorDash, and Airbnb.

BitMine, chaired by Fundstrat’s Tom Lee, has quickly emerged as one of the most talked-about names in the digital asset sector. The company shifted from a Bitcoin mining model to an Ethereum-focused treasury strategy earlier this summer, a move that triggered a dramatic surge in its stock price.

🚀 purchased over $800M of in the past week, adding 190,500 ETH to reach 1.71M total holdings worth $8.23B. — Cryptonews.com (@cryptonews)

Shares of BitMine soared more than 3,000% to a record high of $135 in early July before retreating, though the stock is still up over 400% year-to-date.

The firm now holds 1.71 million ether, worth about $8 billion as of August 24, making it the single largest Ethereum treasury globally. That balance sheet strength has drawn institutional attention, with billionaire investor Peter Thiel taking a 9.1% stake in the company last month.

The endorsement, combined with ARK’s sustained buying, has fueled market speculation that BitMine could become a leading proxy for Ethereum exposure among traditional investors.

The timing of ARK’s latest purchase comes amid heightened volatility in both equities and crypto markets.

Ethereum itself has struggled to break higher in recent weeks after reaching an all-time high, but the strategic bet on BitMine reflects Wood’s long-term focus on decentralized finance as a driver of growth.

Social media reaction from traders and crypto enthusiasts has framed the move as a bullish sign, pointing to BitMine’s role as a key corporate holder of ether.

The ARK Innovation ETF, the firm’s flagship fund with $6.8 billion in assets under management, still counts Tesla as its largest position at 9.7% but has increasingly pivoted toward crypto-related firms.

Alongside BitMine, it maintains sizable stakes in Coinbase, Circle, and Robinhood, which it recently augmented with a $14.2 million purchase.BitMine Becomes World’s Largest Corporate Ethereum Holder With $8.25B Stash

BitMine Immersion Technologies has intensified its buying spree, pushing its Ethereum holdings to unprecedented levels.

The Delaware-based firm disclosed it now controls 1,713,899 ETH, valued at $8.25 billion, alongside 192 Bitcoin and $562 million in cash.

The position cements BitMine as the world’s largest corporate Ethereum treasury and places it second among all corporate crypto holders, behind only Michael Saylor’s Strategy Inc.

The firm’s accumulation strategy, launched in late June, has added more than 190,000 ETH in a single week.

Crypto investor led the scale of buying “absolutely unprecedented,” noting that BitMine has absorbed 1.4% of all ETH supply in just 50 days.

“If Tom [Lee] gets to his 5% target and ETH goes above $12,000, BitMine would be larger than MicroStrategy,” Adams said.

Absolutely unprecedented.Tom Lee has acquired almost $10 billion ETH over the past 50 days.Over 1.7m ETH.1.4% of all ETH supply.If Tom gets to his 5% target and ETH goes above $12k Bitmine would be larger than Microstrategy. — RYAN SΞAN ADAMS – rsa.eth 🦄 (@RyanSAdams)

To fuel further purchases, BitMine has dramatically expanded its fundraising. On August 12, the company filed with the SEC to increase its at-the-market equity offering to $24.5 billion, up from the $2 billion announced in July.

The offering, led by Cantor Fitzgerald and ThinkEquity, represents one of the largest equity raise expansions in the crypto sector.

While proceeds may also support Bitcoin acquisitions and mining operations, Ethereum remains the company’s clear priority. As of early August, BitMine controlled roughly 5% of ETH’s circulating supply, surpassing other corporate treasuries within just 35 days of launching its strategy.

Fundstrat CIO Tom Lee projects Ethereum could reach $5,500 in the near term and potentially $12,000 by year-end, citing Wall Street’s growing interest following the passage of the GENIUS Stablecoin Act. With more than $145 billion in stablecoins running on Ethereum, Lee described ETH as one of the most important macro investments of the decade.

data.title: Lantronix Is Maintained at Buy by Lake Street
Beef and nut prices have surged - and that's driving Hormel's stock to its biggest drop ever
data.description:
By Steve Gelsi

The parent of Spam and Planters brands said beef, pork and nut markets have 'worsened significantly beyond our projection'

Spam and Skippy maker Hormel warned of a fiscal fourth-quarter profit shortfall on rising input costs for meat and other commodities.

Hormel Foods Corp.'s stock could see the steepest one-day loss in its trading history on Thursday after the maker of Spam, Planters nuts and Jennie-O turkey slashed its profit outlook due to higher-than-expected costs for meat and nuts.

Hormel's stock (HRL) was down 13.5% to $25.11 a share, which will mark its lowest finish in about 10 years if the losses hold through the close, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Hormel said it's taking "targeted pricing actions" in the face of beef, pork and nut markets that have "worsened significantly beyond our projection."

For example, the price of pork bellies - the underside of the animal used to make bacon - rose 30%, the company said.

Beef prices, which have posed a challenge due to lower supply, are "a persistent inflationary headwind industry-wide and near [an] all-time high," Chief Financial Officer Jacinth Smiley said on a conference call Thursday following the company's quarterly earnings report, according to a transcript.

Hormel warned it now expects fiscal fourth-quarter earnings of 38 cents to 40 cents a share, which is below the FactSet consensus estimate of 47 cents a share.

Hormel's third-quarter profit rose to $183.74 million, or 33 cents a share, from $176.7 million, or 32 cents a share, in the year-ago quarter.

Its adjusted third-quarter earnings of 35 cents a share missed the FactSet consensus estimate of 40 cents a share. The company said the result was disappointing and fell short of its own expectations.

Revenue rose 5% to $3.03 billion, coming in ahead of the Wall Street consensus estimate of $2.98 billion.

Hormel said its results included "strong organic volume and net sales performance" despite headwinds on its profit.

Jennie-O turkey "delivered another impressive quarter" due to consumer demand and improved distribution, the company said.

Hormel also invested in Spam by launching singles portions in flavors including bacon and hot and spicy.

In its food-service unit, Hormel's premium pepperoni increased volume by more than 20%, but industrywide traffic was down in that sector.

Hormel said its efforts to improve performance at its Planters nut business were resulting in higher sales, but profitability has lagged expectations.

But the biggest factor in the company's profit weakness was rising input prices.

"That run-up in commodity markets was both sudden, and it was major, as it occurred across inputs that are major for our business," said Jeff Ettinger, Hormel's interim chief executive.

Also read: Tyson's beef business facing 'the most challenging market conditions' it has ever seen

-Steve Gelsi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: FDA recommends more monitoring of Alzheimer's patients on Biogen's drug Leqembi
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration said on Thursday it is recommending an additional, earlier magnetic resonance imaging monitoring prior to the third infusion for patients with Alzheimer's disease taking Biogen's drug Leqembi.

The earlier monitoring can identify individuals with amyloid-related imaging abnormalities with edema (ARIA-E), which is characterized by brain swelling or fluid buildup.

The current prescribing information recommends MRI imaging before the fifth, seventh and 14th infusions.

The FDA said it identified six deaths early in treatment, which prompted an in-depth analysis of serious and fatal outcomes related to ARIA-E before the fifth Leqembi infusion.

Biogen did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

data.title: Polish F-16 crashes during airshow rehearsal
data.description:
A Polish Air Force F-16 fighter jet crashed during a rehearsal for an airshow in Radom, central Poland, and the pilot died, a government spokesman said on Thursday.

Polish media said the aircraft crashed into the runway around 1730 GMT and damaged it. The Radom Airshow planned for the weekend has been cancelled.

Government spokesman Adam Szlapka confirmed the pilot's death on social media platform X but gave no further details.

The defence minister is on his way to the crash site, he added.

Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1416 ET - Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey maker Brown-Forman sees weakness in the U.S. market for spirits as slightly more cyclical than structural, CEO Lawson Whiting says on a call with analysts. Inflation and high interest rates are still hurting customers' ability to spend, and tariffs are putting pressure on them as well, Whiting says. Structural considerations-like the rising popularity of cannabis, more use of GLP-1 drugs and a general wellness push among consumers--wouldn't be able to tank the alcohol market as quickly as has been seen, the CEO says. "There is some continuum between cyclical and structural," he says. "But I still feel like the cyclical side is a little bit bigger piece of it." Shares retreat 5.1% to $28.89. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1211 ET - Gold futures rise on mounting bets of a September U.S. interest-rate cut. Futures are up 0.55% at $3,467.70 a troy ounce. The precious metal has made steady gains since Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech last Friday, in which he opened the door to easing monetary policy, Trade Nation's David Morrison says in a note. Investors are also keeping a close eye on the weaker U.S. dollar, which is currently influencing gold prices to an extent, Morrison says. A weaker dollar is less competitive with gold's safe-haven aspects and makes it cheaper for international investors to buy dollar-denominated bullion. In regards to the dollar, upcoming data releases including core personal consumption expenditure data on Friday should keep the market on alert, Morrison says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1140 ET - U.S. activity and labor indicators do little to change the prospect of an interest rate cut by the Fed next month, and Treasury yields weaken with some curve flattening. GDP in the 2Q expands by an annualized rate of 3.3%, while jobless claims decline a little. Economists expect both output growth and job creation to slow in coming months. August payrolls are due next week, with some forecasters calling for an uptick in unemployment. The 10-year Treasury yield is at 4.216%, after settling yesterday at 4.238%. The two-year rises to 3.637% from 3.624%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1112 ET - Credit spreads between French bank bonds and Italian bank bonds narrow as Italian bonds perform better than their French equivalents, ING's Maureen Schuller says in a note. "Italian bank bonds have fared well by the reduced sovereign concerns and related positive rating momentum," she says. Credit spreads between the two asset classes are likely to widen once political stability is restored in France, Schuller says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1055 ET - The potential collapse of the French government is unlikely to prove a sustained driver of the euro for the remainder of the year, Deutsche Bank's George Saravelos says in a note. "The big picture this year is that despite all the political noise, France has had a de facto coalition government that not only managed to reach agreement on a very tough 2025 budget but also is actually delivering on the tightening that was agreed." Unless the news flow shifts to suggest a far more adverse fiscal outcome, French politics are unlikely to have major impact on the euro-dollar exchange rate in coming months, he says. The euro rises 0.3% to $1.1672. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1043 ET - The cost of celebrating a friend's big day now rivals the price of keeping a roof over your head, Zillow says. A new Zillow analysis finds that attending just one wedding and a bachelor or bachelorette weekend can set you back about $2,010, comparable to the typical U.S. monthly rent of $2,072. In fact, celebrating a single wedding costs more than a month of rent in nearly 70% of the largest U.S. metros. Nearly half of Gen Z and millennials--45%--say they've made a housing sacrifice to afford these celebrations. The most common trade-offs include renting or buying a smaller home and living with roommates. One-quarter say they've declined to attend at least one wedding event because of the cost. The median age of renters who moved in the past year is 33, nearly identical to the U.S. median age of marriage of 32. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1005 ET - Falling mortgage rates have pushed the median U.S. monthly mortgage payment down to $2,616, its lowest level since the start of the year, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate is sitting at 6.58%, its lowest level in 10 months. Lower housing costs are bringing some house hunters off the sidelines. Pending home sales rose 1.6% year over year during the four weeks ending Wednesday. That marks two straight months of increases in pending sales after they fell for most of 2025. But many buyers are still waiting on the sidelines. A lot of prospective buyers are waiting to see if the Fed cuts interest rates in September to buy. But Redfin economists say today's mortgage rates likely already reflect the upcoming rate cut. On the selling side, new listings are up 1.9% year over year. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0941 ET - Payroll numbers in Canada retreated in June and job vacancies edged higher, Statistics Canada data shows. The number of employees receiving pay and benefits from an employer fell 32,900, or 0.2% on-month, to more than take back the 18,500 rise in payroll employment the month before. Declines were seen in manufacturing, retail trade, construction and health care. At the same time, job vacancies were up 12,100, or 2.5%, to take back some of the cumulative 37,100 decline in April and May. The closely watched, and more timely, labor force survey showed the unemployment rate in Canada held at 6.9% in July after ticking down 0.1 percentage point the month before, while employment fell by about 41,000 to take back some of June's 83,000 rise. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0901 ET - The Chinese yuan rises to its highest level against the dollar since November 2024 after the People's Bank of China's daily fixing allowed for more strength and as the U.S. currency falls. The PBOC set its reference rate for the dollar-yuan exchange rate at 7.1063 yuan Thursday, down from the previous day's fix of 7.1108. "It seems increasingly clear that Chinese authorities want a stronger renminbi," ING's Chris Turner says in a note. This could mark a move to improve the yuan's status in international trade, he says. It provides a welcoming environment for international capital, while a stronger yuan could boost consumption by preserving the purchasing power of households, he says. The dollar falls to a low of 7.1303 yuan, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0732 ET - Gold futures rise on a weaker U.S. dollar, safe-haven demand and mounting expectations of a September U.S. interest-rate cut. Futures are up 0.4% at $3,461.10 a troy ounce. The precious metal has gained as expectations of looser monetary policy raise the appeal of non-interest bearing bullion, SP Angel analysts say in a note. Market focus will be on Friday's Personal Consumption Expenditure data, with concerns centering around the interplay between unemployment and containing inflation, SP Angel says. The market currently expects an 88% chance of a 25 basis-point rate cut in September. Investor concerns over the Federal Reserve's independence from the White House following President Trump's move to oust Governor Lisa Cook might be feeding gold's rally, though steady gains in U.S. Treasuries suggest fears aren't yet overbearing, SP Angel adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0716 ET - The Norwegian krone should strengthen as the country's economic growth outperforms peers and a broader European recovery comes to fruition, UBS Global Wealth Management strategists say in a note. Recent data reinforce the view that Norway's "underlying economic fundamentals are solid compared to other G-10 economies exposed to growth downside risks due to tariffs," they say. Fiscal stimulus is expected to support a European recovery. Moreover, the Norges Bank sounded cautious about cutting interest rates at its latest meeting and didn't signal a cut in September. That leaves room for markets to price out rate cuts, the strategists say. UBS expects the euro to fall to 11.0 kroner by September 2026 from 11.7480 kroner currently. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0640 ET - The cost of insuring French bank bonds against default stays high as investors exercise caution amid political uncertainty. The French political stalemate "hits markets directly because it ties into the country's rising debt," Swissquote Bank's Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. BNP Paribas's five-year credit default swaps climbed to a two-month high of 45 basis points on Wednesday and remain at that level, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. Societe Generale's CDS rise 1bp to 51bps. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1416 ET - Jack Daniel's Tennessee Whiskey maker Brown-Forman sees weakness in the U.S. market for spirits as slightly more cyclical than structural, CEO Lawson Whiting says on a call with analysts. Inflation and high interest rates are still hurting customers' ability to spend, and tariffs are putting pressure on them as well, Whiting says. Structural considerations-like the rising popularity of cannabis, more use of GLP-1 drugs and a general wellness push among consumers--wouldn't be able to tank the alcohol market as quickly as has been seen, the CEO says. "There is some continuum between cyclical and structural," he says. "But I still feel like the cyclical side is a little bit bigger piece of it." Shares retreat 5.1% to $28.89. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1329 ET - Five Below shoppers are responding well to the discount retailer's price increases, UBS analyst Michael Lasser says. Normally, customers would gradually start buying or visiting less after a retailer makes mass price changes, Lasser says. But Five Below has actually received more visits and spending over the past few months since it initiated price hikes in April and May in response to tariffs. Lasser thinks that's because customers are recognizing the widening price gaps between Five Below and other retailers, as Five Below still hasn't passed the full cost of tariffs down to sticker prices. That leaves Five Below with room to potentially raise prices further. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1300 ET - Pure Storage's strong 2Q report reflects widespread growth across categories, but it's tough to find an exact catalyst for the results, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note. "Our only conundrum is we can't point to any exact factors that are catalyzing PSTG's outperformance now specifically," the analysts say, noting competitor NetApp's relatively disappointing results suggest any pickup in storage sales likely isn't broad based. Nevertheless, with better trends across the board, the analysts raise their price target on Pure Storage's stock to $80 from $70. Shares rise 31% to $79.62. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1249 ET - Industry brand loyalty rates declined slightly in the first half of 2025 following last year's rebound, according to a S&P Global Mobility analysis of new vehicle registration data June. The industry's brand loyalty rate through June stood at 51.1%, down 1.4 percentage points from the same period in 2024. The decrease marks a reversal from 2024's year-over-year gain, showing continued volatility in loyalty trends as the market continues to stabilize post-pandemic. Both mainstream and luxury brands contributed to the decline. The number of households returning to market rose 4.2% year-over-year, representing the third consecutive year of improvement. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1235 ET - Pure Storage continued to make progress on its engagement with Meta and other hyperscaler customers in its latest quarter, setting the stage for a key long-term growth driver, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. Pure Storage management noted Meta initiated its first volume deployments, resulting in the first revenue contributions from the collaboration, and the company is looking to enable its other hyperscaler customers to replace raw storage with its DirectFlash Modules. The analysts say revenue contributions from such engagements will be minimal this fiscal year, but will be a key component of expectations for further accelerating growth in the future. They raise their price target on the stock to $80 from $70. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1226 ET - Williams-Sonoma continues to post solid results against a backdrop of challenging demand following the pandemic and rapidly increasing tariffs, but the market is unlikely to reward the company's impressive performance, say Oppenheimer analysts. Investors are unlikely to assign Williams-Sonoma shares a higher multiple that better reflects its adept navigation of a challenging period without an improvement in the macroeconomic environment and the housing market, the analysts say. Once those broader economic factors pick up, the company will have a "clearer path towards sustained, outsized, top-line expansion," the analysts say. Shares fall 2.3% to 187.79. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1218 ET - Dick's Sporting Goods shares are under pressure after the company, while raising its full-year outlook, pulled back the throttle on its gross margin target. It previously guided for gross margin to expand by 75 basis points, but now the expectation is that gross margin will simply be up compared with fiscal 2024. When asked about the softened view on an analyst call, CFO Navdeep Gupta says Dick's is "balancing several puts and takes" as it navigates tariffs, its own focus on keeping inventory vibrant and pricing in a dynamic promotional environment. "We're trying to balance all of those different drivers as we look to the back half and balance that against our SG&A expectations from an investment perspective," Gupta says. Shares fall 5% to $214.78. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1211 ET - Williams-Sonoma may have to raise prices to offset rising tariffs, but it will likely do so selectively, identifying products whose demand is least sensitive to higher prices, say UBS analysts. For new or highly unique products, customers will have a higher willingness to pay up and also have fewer comparisons for what the previous prices of these products were. "It's likely harder to take prices up on items that are more commoditized, such as a plain bath towel," the analysts say. Williams-Sonoma's customers are also likely less sensitive to price changes with its average customer household income around $140,000. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1205 ET - Pure Storage is now on track to accelerate growth this year and beyond after seeing significant revenue momentum in its latest quarter, helped by broad-based strength across its portfolio, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company's raised guidance reflects its growing confidence in customers' spending intentions as the underlying macroeconomic environment remains more resilient than feared earlier in the year, the analysts say. Pure Storage's revenue performance and boosted outlook stand out relative to peers with more modest upsides and consistent outlooks, they say, noting such competitors are more exposed to a pullback in public sector spending. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1157 ET - Williams-Sonoma maintained its operating margin guidance for the year despite its tariff rate doubling from May, which is impressive, say UBS analysts. But even before factoring additional furniture tariff rates that President Trump has threatened, tariff impacts should grow as the year progresses. The company's accounting method delays the impact of higher product costs, while it pulled forward some of its inventory in the first half of the year, subjecting it to lower tariffs. Assuming Williams-Sonoma has to absorb 15% to 30% of tariff costs, it could see a 135 to 260 basis point hit to margins, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1128 ET - Dick's Sporting Goods sees a tremendous opportunity to turn around the Foot Locker business that it's planning to acquire next month, though it can't provide many details while the transaction remains pending, CEO Lauren Hobart says on a call with analysts. Management is working with Dick's brand partners, who are excited about the opportunity to turn Foot Locker around and already sharing strong insights, Hobart says. Dick's also plans to invest in stores, invest in marketing and bring in a new assortment of products for Foot Locker, the CEO says, but can't provide more details ahead of the planned Sept. 8 close of the deal. "We will be back to you at our Q3 call with more specific details," she says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1114ET - Dick's Sporting Goods raised its full-year guidance after a strong 2Q, but the market may have been looking for a bigger lift than the 10-cent boost it gave to the earnings outlook, Citi analysts say in a research note. Management also boosted its guidance for comparable sales growth to 2% to 3.5%, which implies that the second half of the year's comparable sales will be 2% to 3% higher. That's below the 5% comps gain in the first half of the year, which was better than what management had been forecasting, so the second-half guidance looks conservative, the analysts say. They expect shares to trade flat today as the company's strong 2Q results are offset by its "only-modest" guidance raise. Shares slide 6% to $212.42 (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Cooper Cos. Continues Slide After Cut to Fiscal-Year Revenue View
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Shares of Cooper Cos. slipped a day after the company lowered its outlook for fiscal-year revenue and posted third-quarter earnings that disappointed analysts.

The stock was down 12%, to $65.53, midday Thursday and has dropped 29% so far this year.

The company on Wednesday trimmed its fiscal-year revenue guidance, to $4.08 billion to $4.1 billion from $4.11 billion to $4.15 billion. Analysts pointed to weaker sales trends for its Clariti contact lens brand and softening e-commerce trends in the Asia-Pacific region.

The San Ramon, Calif., company beat analysts' earnings estimates for the most recent quarter, but in a research note, Stifel analysts said that most of the earnings beat was from below-the-line items including a favorable tax rate.

Sales for the company's CooperVision segment were $718.4 million, below analysts' expectations of $720.6 million, according to FactSet.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Consumer Discretionary Stocks Are Back in Fashion. There's a Case for More Gains. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Teresa Rivas

Monday's Labor Day holiday brings the unofficial end of summer, a bittersweet moment that many people take as a signal to indulge in some retail therapy. A recent resurgent in consumer stocks indicates the shopping may have already begun.

It is a departure from how the picture looked earlier in the year.

While spending remained surprisingly resilient despite the years of inflation that followed the pandemic, consumer confidence took a major hit earlier this year. President Donald Trump's introduction of stiff tariffs, the resultant stock market selloff, and concern about a weakening economy spelled trouble for retailers.

The SPDR S&P Retail exchange-traded fund, trading under the ticker XRT, declined nearly 22% from the start of the year through the April 8 post-Liberation Day lows, while the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF, or XLY, lost about 26%. Those numbers compare with a decline of roughly 15% for the S&P 500.

Now, the consumer discretionary sector is doing just fine. Despite a slight dip in August, consumer sentiment improved this summer from its lows in the spring. Both XRT and XLY have been reaching record levels in recent days, alongside the broader market.

Amazon.com, meanwhile, has yet to close above its early February high of $242.

Renaissance Macro Research founder and Chairman Jeffrey deGraaf highlights this trend from a technical perspective in a new note Thursday, noting that XRT "has made a modest relative strength breakout as it trades at new absolute highs." Relative strength, which refers to a security or index performing better than comparable assets, can be a signal that price gains are coming.

It isn't just shopping sprees that are boosting consumer stocks. "Auto and components have turned positive in our relative work while cruises, hotels and some casinos start to catch a bid," deGraaf writes. He said he is still cautious about restaurants because deteriorating relative trends lead him to believe the stocks will see increased volatility and achieve lower returns.

Consumer discretionary, though, has room to rise to catch up with the broader market's gains so far this year. The S&P 500 closed at its 19th record high of 2025 on Wednesday, putting it up more than 10% year to date. By contrast, XRT is up about 8% and XLY — Amazon is its largest holding, at 23% of the fund — is up roughly 4%.

Some of the gains may be the result of hope that Americans will splash out on Labor Day sales as a last hurrah before tariffs send prices higher. The likelihood that they will continue could be an antidote to the end-of-summer blues.

Write to Teresa Rivas at teresa.rivas@barrons.com

BEWI ASA: Mandatory notification of trade and disclosure of large shareholding - redelivery of borrowed shares in Tranche 1 of the Private Placement
data.description:
BEWI ASA

Reference is made to the stock exchange announcement made by BEWI ASA ("BEWI" or the "Company") on 20 August 2025 regarding the successful private placement raising gross proceeds of NOK 896 million, equivalent to EUR 75 million (the "Private Placement"). Reference is further made to the mandatory notification of trade and disclosure of large shareholding on 21 August 2025, regarding the lending of 21,039,345 shares in the Company by BEWI Invest AS to DNB Carnegie, a part of DNB Bank ASA, acting as settlement agent on behalf of the Managers, for the purpose of facilitating delivery of allocated shares in Tranche 1 of the Private Placement.

Following registration of the share capital increase for Tranche 1 in the Private Placement, BEWI's share capital is NOK 230,066,748, divided into 230,066,748 shares, each with a par value of NOK 1.00 (prior to the issuance of the Tranche 2 shares in the Private Placement).

DNB Carnegie has today redelivered 21,039,345 shares in the Company to BEWI Invest AS, as settlement of the borrowed shares described above. Following the redelivery of shares, BEWI Invest AS' shareholding in BEWI has increased to 115,263,441 shares (representing ~50.10% of the issued shares and votes). BEWI Invest AS does not have any rights to further shares or votes in the Company.

Please see further details in the attached notification of trade.

This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to the Market Abuse Regulation (EU) 596/2014 (MAR) article 19 number 3 and sections 4-2 and 5-12 of the Norwegian Securities Trading Act.

data.title: Gold Rises to 5-Week High on Softer Dollar
data.description:
Gold prices climbed above $3,415 per ounce on Thursday, reaching a five-week high as a weaker dollar and safe-haven demand lifted the metal.

A 0.4% drop in the dollar made bullion more affordable for overseas buyers, bolstering demand.

Prices have been climbing for more than a week, fueled by concerns that political pressure on the Federal Reserve could prompt faster and deeper rate cuts.

Markets are now pricing in an 87% chance of a quarter-point reduction in September, with attention turning to Friday’s PCE inflation report for further guidance.

Uncertainty also grew after Fed Governor Lisa Cook filed a lawsuit challenging Trump’s authority to remove her, highlighting risks to central bank independence.

Meanwhile, jobless claims edged lower, but signs of weakening labor market momentum and the prospect of higher unemployment are strengthening expectations of a more dovish Fed stance, adding support for gold.
